flashcards.definition: Capturing implemented changes in code is important as it will allow for a quick reversion to a known good state of the code if a change causes problems. flashcards.definition: In a fail-open mode, if the IPS fails, it will still allow traffic to pass through, maintaining network connectivity. In a fail-closed mode, if the IPS fails, it will block all traffic, disrupting network connectivity.

flashcards.hint: A security team is reviewing a failure mode that permits access when a control fails.
flashcards.front: A security team is reviewing a failure mode that permits access when a control fails.
flashcards.keyDetails: In a fail-closed mode, if the IPS fails, it will block all traffic, disrupting network connectivity. Active mode is also an IPS operation mode, not a failure mode. In active mode, the IPS monitors traffic and blocks threats. A fail-open mode ensures traffic flow if the IPS fails. Passive mode is an IPS operation mode, not a failure mode.
flashcards.example: A security team is reviewing a failure mode that permits access when a control fails.
flashcards.memoryHook: Link "Fail-open" to: in a fail-open mode, if the ips fails, it will still allow traffic to pass through, maintaining network connectivity.

flashcards.keyDetails: Companies without these protections in place would have a hard time dealing with constant DoS attacks. An on-path attack occurs when a malicious user has found a way to intercept data transmitted before it arrives to the intended recipient. The capabilities added to the system would not protect against this attack.
flashcards.example: A company prepares to add additional protection to its networks due to ongoing attacks.

flashcards.definition: Phishing is a combination of social engineering and spoofing. It persuades or tricks the target into interacting with a malicious resource disguised as a trusted one.
topics: 2.2 Explain common threat vectors and attack surfaces.
flashcards.hint: A security team is reviewing a social-engineering attack commonly delivered through deceptive email.
flashcards.front: A security team is reviewing a social-engineering attack commonly delivered through deceptive email.
flashcards.keyDetails: It persuades or tricks the target into interacting with a malicious resource disguised as a trusted one. Typosquatting means that the threat actor registers a domain name very similar to a real one, hoping that users will not notice the difference and assume they are browsing a trusted site.
flashcards.example: A security team is reviewing a social-engineering attack commonly delivered through deceptive email.
flashcards.memoryHook: Link "Phishing" to: phishing is a combination of social engineering and spoofing.

flashcards.definition: Vishing is voice-based phishing that uses phone calls or voice messages to manipulate victims.

flashcards.hint: A security team is reviewing a social-engineering attack conducted through voice calls.
flashcards.front: A security team is reviewing a social-engineering attack conducted through voice calls.

flashcards.definition: Pretexting is social engineering that uses a fabricated scenario or identity to gain trust and extract information or access.
topics: 2.2 Explain common threat vectors and attack surfaces.

flashcards.definition: Impersonation simply means pretending to be someone else. Impersonation is possible when the target cannot easily verify the attacker's identity, such as over the phone or via email.
topics: 2.2 Explain common threat vectors and attack surfaces.
flashcards.hint: is a social engineering attack that involves which of the following?
flashcards.front: is a social engineering attack that involves which of the following?
flashcards.keyDetails: This option describes a pharming attack, not impersonation. Pharming manipulates Domain Name System (DNS) settings to redirect users to fraudulent websites. This option refers to a phishing attack, not impersonation. Phishing uses deceptive emails to trick users into revealing sensitive information.
flashcards.example: is a social engineering attack that involves which of the following?
flashcards.memoryHook: Link "Impersonation" to: impersonation simply means pretending to be someone else.

flashcards.definition: Typosquatting means that the threat actor registers a domain name very similar to a real one, hoping that users will not notice the difference and assume they are browsing a trusted site. Phishing is a combination of social engineering and spoofing.
topics: 2.2 Explain common threat vectors and attack surfaces.
flashcards.hint: A security team is reviewing registering look-alike domain names based on common typing errors.

flashcards.keyDetails: Phishing is a combination of social engineering and spoofing. It persuades or tricks the target into interacting with a malicious resource disguised as a trusted one. Brand impersonation occurs when the threat actor commits resources to accurately duplicating a company's logos and formatting to make a phishing message or pharming website visually compelling.
flashcards.example: A security team is reviewing registering look-alike domain names based on common typing errors.
flashcards.memoryHook: Link "Typosquatting" to: typosquatting means that the threat actor registers a domain name very similar to a real one, hoping that users will not notice the difference and assume they are browsing a trusted site.

flashcards.definition: A watering-hole attack compromises a site commonly used by a target group so visitors from that group are exposed to malware or credential theft.

flashcards.definition: Removable media is a threat vector in which USB drives or other portable storage introduce malware, unauthorized data transfer, or data loss.
topics: 2.2 Explain common threat vectors and attack surfaces.
flashcards.hint: A group of hackers has been monitoring recent orders from a company involving new laptops and Universal Serial Bus (USB) thumb drives. The group infiltrated the shipping company and added malicious USB thumb drives to the order.
flashcards.front: A group of hackers has been monitoring recent orders from a company involving new laptops and Universal Serial Bus (USB) thumb drives. The group infiltrated the shipping company and added malicious USB thumb drives to the order.
flashcards.keyDetails: Malicious actors can replace parts of the laptop or hack the operating systems before it gets to the company. Adding malicious USB thumb drives to the order takes advantage of removable media to trick the user into plugging them into a computer where the hacker can carry out further attacks.
flashcards.example: A group of hackers has been monitoring recent orders from a company involving new laptops and Universal Serial Bus (USB) thumb drives.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Removable" + "media" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Default credentials are vendor-supplied usernames or passwords that must be changed before systems or devices are used in production.
topics: 2.3 Explain various types of vulnerabilities.

flashcards.keyDetails: A phishing attack involves deceiving users to reveal their sensitive information, but in this scenario, the attack does not involve tricking users or obtaining credentials through deceptive means.

flashcards.definition: A denial-of-service (DoS) attack attempts to make a system, service, or network unavailable to legitimate users.

flashcards.definition: A distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack floods a target from multiple sources, often botnets, to degrade or deny service.

flashcards.hint: A major online retail company has recently been experiencing intermittent downtime of its website. Network analysts observe a massive influx of traffic from multiple sources to the server. However, the traffic seems redirected from other systems.

flashcards.keyDetails: Collision is mainly used in the context of cryptographic hash functions and does not relate to the influx of traffic causing downtime. Injection attacks occur when an attacker sends harmful data to a system, tricking it into executing unintended commands. They typically do not result in a massive influx of traffic.

flashcards.definition: A worm is a standalone malware program that replicates itself to spread to other computers, often over a network. Worms consume bandwidth and system resources, slowing down or even crashing systems.

flashcards.definition: Polymorphic malware changes its code or appearance to evade signature-based detection while preserving its function.
topics: 2.4 Given a scenario, analyze indicators of malicious activity.
flashcards.hint: A new virus is infecting computers on a network segment. Containment is difficult because the virus changes its code.
flashcards.front: A new virus is infecting computers on a network segment. Containment is difficult because the virus changes its code.
flashcards.keyDetails: A trojan virus is malware inside another file that appears legitimate. When the legitimate file executes, the trojan comes out and infects the system. A boot virus is one in which the virus code writes to the disk boot sector or the partition table of a fixed disk. The virus executes each time the operating system (OS) starts.

flashcards.definition: A Remote Access Trojan (RAT) is malware that gives an attacker remote control over an infected system while disguising itself as legitimate software or activity.
topics: 2.4 Given a scenario, analyze indicators of malicious activity.
flashcards.hint: A workstation repeatedly communicates with an unknown external IP address. Unauthorized software appears to be transmitting sensitive system data and receiving commands or files without the user noticing.
flashcards.front: A workstation repeatedly communicates with an unknown external IP address. Unauthorized software appears to be transmitting sensitive system data and receiving commands or files without the user noticing.

flashcards.definition: Ransomware is a virus that infects a computer and threatens to perform actions unless the user pays a ransom before a particular deadline. Crypto-ransomware specifically requires monetary payment before destroying the key that would allow the decryption of the user's files.
topics: 2.4 Given a scenario, analyze indicators of malicious activity.
flashcards.hint: A personal computer locks after receiving a file. The screen shows a countdown clock and threatens permanent file encryption unless money is paid.
flashcards.front: A personal computer locks after receiving a file. The screen shows a countdown clock and threatens permanent file encryption unless money is paid.
flashcards.keyDetails: Crypto-ransomware specifically encrypts files or threatens loss of the decryption key unless payment is made. Also known as: Crypto-ransomware.
flashcards.example: A personal computer locks after receiving a file. The screen shows a countdown clock and threatens permanent file encryption unless money is paid.
flashcards.memoryHook: Link "Ransomware" to: ransomware is a virus that infects a computer and threatens to perform actions unless the user pays a ransom before a particular deadline.

flashcards.definition: Spyware is software that secretly collects information about users, systems, or activity without proper consent.
topics: 2.4 Given a scenario, analyze indicators of malicious activity.
flashcards.hint: A user's browser redirects unexpectedly, pop-ups appear frequently, and the SOC later confirms credential theft from the endpoint.
flashcards.front: A user's browser redirects unexpectedly, pop-ups appear frequently, and the SOC later confirms credential theft from the endpoint.
flashcards.keyDetails: 2.0 Threats, Vulnerabilities, and Mitigations.

flashcards.memoryHook: Link "Spyware" to: spyware is software that secretly collects information about users, systems, or activity without proper consent.

flashcards.definition: A hardware keylogger is a physical device placed between a keyboard and computer, or embedded in a keyboard, to capture keystrokes.

flashcards.front: During a desk inspection, a small unknown device is found between a keyboard cable and the workstation.

flashcards.definition: A potentially unwanted program (PUP) is software that may be installed alongside desired software or preinstalled on a system and may create privacy, performance, or security concerns.
topics: 2.4 Given a scenario, analyze indicators of malicious activity.
flashcards.hint: An IT intern prepares and connects used laptops to the company network. The intern discovers that some of the laptops have degraded performance and decreased storage. What is commonly associated with s? (Select the two best options.)
flashcards.front: An IT intern prepares and connects used laptops to the company network. The intern discovers that some of the laptops have degraded performance and decreased storage. What is commonly associated with s? (Select the two best options.)
flashcards.keyDetails: Potentially unwanted programs (PUPs) are sometimes described as grayware rather than malware and can also be referred to as bloatware. A trojan is a form of malware that is concealed within an installer package for software that appears to be legitimate.
flashcards.example: An IT intern prepares and connects used laptops to the company network.

flashcards.definition: Buffer overflow vulnerabilities occur when an application receives more data than it can handle, resulting in the potential for unintended command execution. Privilege escalation refers to an attacker gaining unauthorized privileges on a system, usually through exploiting system misconfigurations or vulnerabilities that allow them to elevate their permissions.

flashcards.hint: A security team is reviewing writing beyond an allocated memory boundary.
flashcards.front: A security team is reviewing writing beyond an allocated memory boundary.

flashcards.definition: An application-level vulnerability is a weakness in application code, configuration, dependencies, or distribution that can be exploited.
topics: 2.3 Explain various types of vulnerabilities.
flashcards.hint: A company has recently deployed a new mobile application for its employees. During a security audit, observations show some employees downloaded the application from third- party app stores, not the official ones.
flashcards.front: A company has recently deployed a new mobile application for its employees. During a security audit, observations show some employees downloaded the application from third- party app stores, not the official ones.
flashcards.keyDetails: Using older versions of an operating system could expose the devices to vulnerabilities patched in later versions, leading to operating system-level vulnerabilities. The scenario does not mention any outdated hardware. The issue is with the operating system versions and the source of the application download.

flashcards.memoryHook: "Application-level" + "vulnerability" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Downloading applications from third-party app stores increases the risk of installing versions that someone may have tampered with or may contain malicious code, introducing application-level vulnerabilities. Using older versions of an operating system could expose the devices to vulnerabilities patched in later versions, leading to operating system-level vulnerabilities.
topics: 2.3 Explain various types of vulnerabilities.
flashcards.hint: Downloading applications from third-party app stores increases the risk of installing versions that someone may have tampered with or may contain malicious code, introducing application-level vulnerabilities.

flashcards.definition: A legacy system is an older system or application that remains in use but may be difficult or impossible to patch, integrate, or secure.

flashcards.hint: Companies that have existed for a long time typically employ many different types of servers and software.
flashcards.front: Companies that have existed for a long time typically employ many different types of servers and software.

flashcards.definition: Input validation checks user input against expected type, length, format, range, and syntax before processing it.

flashcards.definition: A Certificate Authority (CA) or owner can revoke or suspend a certificate for many reasons. A Certificate Revocation List (CRL) is a list of no longer valid certificates.

flashcards.definition: A honeytoken is a fake credential, record, API key, or data item whose use indicates suspicious or unauthorized activity.

flashcards.definition: The continuity of operations plan (COOP) focuses on the critical processes that must remain operational during and after a substantial disruption, like a natural disaster. flashcards.keyDetails: Malicious actors can replace parts of the laptop or hack the operating systems before it gets to the company. Adding malicious USB thumb drives to the order takes advantage of removable media to trick the user into plugging them into a computer where the hacker can carry out further attacks.
flashcards.example: A group of hackers has been monitoring recent orders from a company involving new laptops and Universal Serial Bus (USB) thumb drives.

flashcards.definition: Standards are mandatory or expected requirements that define how policies must be implemented in a consistent, measurable way.

flashcards.keyDetails: Following the standard for each build would ensure each server's configuration would match. Access control models define appropriate access models for different use cases. It does not deal with the configuration of servers. Organizational policies are vital in establishing effective governance and ensuring organizational compliance.

flashcards.definition: Automation executes repeatable tasks with minimal human effort, while orchestration coordinates multiple automated tasks into a larger workflow.

flashcards.definition: Centralized computing architecture refers to a model where all data processing and storage is performed in a single location, typically a central server. That can help minimize threat vectors.
topics: 3.2 Given a scenario, apply security principles to secure enterprise infrastructure.

flashcards.front: A network architect at a global financial institution overhauls the company's on-premises network to enhance security and reduce the attack surface.
flashcards.keyDetails: That can help minimize threat vectors. Peer-to-peer (P2P) networks are a derivative of decentralized architecture that is designed to distribute processing and data storage among participating nodes, versus relying on a central server. Content delivery networks distribute content across multiple servers to improve performance, reliability, and scalability.
flashcards.example: A network architect at a global financial institution overhauls the company's on-premises network to enhance security and reduce the attack surface.

flashcards.definition: Checksum verification not only assures the "Integrity" aspect of the CIA triad but also significantly bolsters data integrity by minimizing the risk of data corruption. User access controls are fundamental mechanisms that limit and control access to information systems.

flashcards.term: Cloud architectures actively delegate security tasks between cloud service providers and customers, creating a shared responsibility model.
flashcards.definition: One of the defining features of cloud architectures is the shared responsibility model, where both the cloud service provider and the customer have specific, delineated security roles. In traditional architectures, with physical devices often stored onsite, there is a critical need to ensure robust physical access controls to prevent unauthorized interventions.
topics: 3.2 Given a scenario, apply security principles to secure enterprise infrastructure.
flashcards.hint: One of the defining features of cloud architectures is the shared responsibility model, where both the cloud service provider and the customer have specific, delineated security roles.
flashcards.front: One of the defining features of cloud architectures is the shared responsibility model, where both the cloud service provider and the customer have specific, delineated security roles.
flashcards.keyDetails: In traditional architectures, with physical devices often stored onsite, there is a critical need to ensure robust physical access controls to prevent unauthorized interventions. Both traditional network and cloud architectures emphasize the importance of data encryption during transit. Neither model inherently prioritizes it more than the other.
flashcards.example: A cybersecurity analyst at a large corporation is assessing the security implications of transitioning to a hybrid model that incorporates both traditional network and cloud architectures.

flashcards.definition: Corporate-Owned Business Only (COBO) grants the organization ownership of the device, allowing its use solely for company business, providing the most control and improved security. flashcards.keyDetails: Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) involves employees using their own devices, which poses security risks due to the mix of personal and professional data. Choose Your Own Device (CYOD) offers the organization a list of devices for employees to choose from, providing some control but not as strict as COBO.
flashcards.example: A security team is reviewing endpoint hardening or mitigation options and must choose the best control for the device or system.

flashcards.definition: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) mitigates this risk from a basic key exchange. PFS uses Diffie- Hellman (D-H) key agreement to create ephemeral session keys without using the server's private key.

flashcards.front: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) mitigates this risk from a basic key exchange. flashcards.keyDetails: PFS uses Diffie- Hellman (D-H) key agreement to create ephemeral session keys without using the server's private key. A cryptographic hashing algorithm produces a fixed-length string of bits from an input plaintext that can be of any length. The output is known as a hash or as a message digest.

flashcards.term: Employ network gateways that scrutinize incoming traffic for malicious activity
flashcards.definition: Network gateways that evaluate incoming traffic for potentially harmful activity effectively act as an organization's first line of digital defense, thereby safeguarding the company's internal network resources. flashcards.term: Enhanced control over communication between VLANs.
flashcards.definition: One security advantage of implementing VLANs is the ability to apply access control rules that prevent or permit certain types of communication between VLANs, hence mitigating risks. One of the main purposes of VLANs is to segment the network, meaning devices from different VLANs cannot freely communicate with each other without routing.

flashcards.keyDetails: One of the main purposes of VLANs is to segment the network, meaning devices from different VLANs cannot freely communicate with each other without routing. While VLANs can increase network security by segregating traffic, they do not make it impossible for unauthorized individuals to intercept communications.

flashcards.definition: A serverless architecture involves a cloud provider managing the infrastructure and automatically scaling resources up or down based on demand.

flashcards.keyDetails: A multi-tenant architecture is a cost-effective model by allowing multiple customers to share the same infrastructure, with each customer's data and applications being separated logically between the customers. flashcards.keyDetails: This approach, known as hybrid encryption, combines the strengths of both methods. While symmetric encryption is fast and efficient, it requires a secure method to share the encryption key among all the offices. If this key becomes compromised, the data is vulnerable.

flashcards.term: The network performance can be negatively impacted due to large broadcast domains.
flashcards.definition: With hundreds more devices, the broadcast domain in the star topology would grow significantly. When a host sends a broadcast, all connecting hosts will receive it, which can lead to performance degradation due to the high amount of broadcast traffic.

flashcards.term: A credentialed scan provides login rights for a more thorough analysis of potential vulnerabilities.
flashcards.definition: A credentialed scan comes with a user account that has login rights to various hosts, enabling it to conduct a more in-depth analysis, which is particularly useful in detecting misconfigured applications or security settings. A non-credentialed scan does not have login rights, and its view only includes what the host exposes to an unprivileged user on the network.
topics: 4.3 Explain various activities associated with vulnerability management.
aliases: A credentialed scan provides login rights for a more thorough analysis of potential vulnerabilities.
flashcards.hint: A credentialed scan comes with a user account that has login rights to various hosts, enabling it to conduct a more in-depth analysis, which is particularly useful in detecting misconfigured applications or security settings.
flashcards.front: A credentialed scan comes with a user account that has login rights to various hosts, enabling it to conduct a more in-depth analysis, which is particularly useful in detecting misconfigured applications or security settings.
flashcards.keyDetails: A non-credentialed scan does not have login rights, and its view only includes what the host exposes to an unprivileged user on the network. Given the goal of a thorough and in-depth analysis, a credentialed scan would be more effective because it has more access and can uncover more potential vulnerabilities.
flashcards.example: A cybersecurity analyst for a medium-sized company needs to perform a vulnerability scan that provides an in-depth analysis of potential weaknesses in the company's system, including misconfigured applications and security settings.
flashcards.memoryHook: "A" + "vulnerabilities." → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: A jurisdiction can restrict or prevent processing and storage of data on systems that do not physically reside within that jurisdiction.
flashcards.definition: Data sovereignty is the principle that a jurisdiction can impose restrictions or prevent the processing and storage of data on systems that do not physically reside within that jurisdiction. It often requires organizations to use location-specific storage facilities or cloud services.
topics: 4.5 Given a scenario, modify enterprise capabilities to enhance security.
5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
aliases: A jurisdiction can restrict or prevent processing and storage of data on systems that do not physically reside within that jurisdiction.
flashcards.hint: Data sovereignty is the principle that a jurisdiction can impose restrictions or prevent the processing and storage of data on systems that do not physically reside within that jurisdiction. It often requires organizations to use location-specific storage facilities or cloud services.
flashcards.front: Data sovereignty is the principle that a jurisdiction can impose restrictions or prevent the processing and storage of data on systems that do not physically reside within that jurisdiction. It often requires organizations to use location-specific storage facilities or cloud services.
flashcards.keyDetails: It often requires organizations to use location-specific storage facilities or cloud services. The physical location of a data center often directly determines the jurisdiction and laws applicable to the data stored within it.
flashcards.example: A compliance team is reviewing data handling, privacy, or retention requirements and must choose the best concept.
flashcards.memoryHook: "A" + "jurisdiction." → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Access point logs record network behavior related to wireless access.
topics: 4.5 Given a scenario, modify enterprise capabilities to enhance security.
flashcards.hint: When it comes to monitoring network security, which logs help detect any attempts made by a threat actor to attack a wireless network through disassociation events?
flashcards.front: When it comes to monitoring network security, which logs help detect any attempts made by a threat actor to attack a wireless network through disassociation events?
flashcards.keyDetails: In this scenario, disassociation events recorded in access point logs can indicate a threat actor attempting to attack the wireless network. System logs from routers focus on the operation and status of the router itself, not specifically on recording events related to wireless network attacks.
flashcards.example: When it comes to monitoring network security, which logs help detect any attempts made by a threat actor to attack a wireless network through disassociation events?
flashcards.memoryHook: "Access" + "logs" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Active/Active Clustering is the most suitable for a 24/7 e-commerce business. Both nodes in this setup process the connections concurrently, maximizing the utilization of available resources.

flashcards.keyDetails: Both nodes in this setup process the connections concurrently, maximizing the utilization of available resources. While Active/Passive Clustering provides a degree of resilience, it does not fully utilize the available resources, as one node remains idle unless the active node fails.

flashcards.definition: Advanced Endpoint Protection (AEP) solution with Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) capability would be the most effective. This approach doesn't just attempt to prevent initial execution of threats, but provides real-time and historical visibility into potential compromises and aids in the remediation process.

flashcards.term: Establishing a security perimeter with layered access controls

flashcards.term: It ensures continuous critical operations during disruptions.
flashcards.definition: COOP focuses on maintaining the continuity of an organization's critical operations during unexpected disruptions, ensuring minimal downtime and impact. While data protection is a security concept, it is not the primary objective of COOP.

Network monitoring is essential for security, but it is not the main objective of COOP. This function typically falls under intrusion detection systems (IDS) or intrusion prevention systems (IPS).

flashcards.term: It ensures system functionality during and after disruptions.

flashcards.term: Memory dumps provide information about active processes, registry data, and more.
flashcards.definition: A memory dump captures a snapshot of system memory, providing important information for cybersecurity investigations such as active processes, temporary file systems, registry data, network connections, and cryptographic keys.
topics: 4.1 Given a scenario, apply common security techniques to computing resources.
flashcards.hint: A memory dump captures a snapshot of system memory, providing important information for cybersecurity investigations such as active processes, temporary file systems, registry data, network connections, and cryptographic keys.
flashcards.front: A memory dump captures a snapshot of system memory, providing important information for cybersecurity investigations such as active processes, temporary file systems, registry data, network connections, and cryptographic keys.
flashcards.keyDetails: Memory dumps, while detailed, do not serve as a permanent record for long-term trend analysis but rather for immediate forensic investigation purposes. They provide a snapshot of RAM at one moment, not a history of system usage. Memory dumps are not for recovering lost files from mass storage devices.

flashcards.definition: Modifying the password complexity policy to allow for longer but easily remembered passwords can aid in lowering the number of people saving their passwords insecurely. A password reuse policy would be difficult to enforce, as it requires the individual not to use it for other services, even those not connected to the company.
topics: 4.1 Given a scenario, apply common security techniques to computing resources.
flashcards.hint: An organization is designing access and identity controls and must choose the authentication method or relationship that best fits the requirement.

Password history policies could help limit the insecure use and reuse of passwords. However, decreasing the complexity would allow for new passwords that are easy to remember.
flashcards.example: An organization is designing access and identity controls and must choose the authentication method or relationship that best fits the requirement.

flashcards.definition: The password history attribute keeps track of previously used passwords and prevents employees from using them again, discouraging password recycling. Password length refers to the minimum (and sometimes maximum) length that a password should have.
topics: 4.1 Given a scenario, apply common security techniques to computing resources.
flashcards.hint: An organization is designing access and identity controls and must choose the authentication method or relationship that best fits the requirement.

flashcards.example: An organization is designing access and identity controls and must choose the authentication method or relationship that best fits the requirement.

flashcards.definition: A password reuse policy would be difficult to enforce, as it requires the individual not to use it for other services, even those not connected to the company.
topics: 4.1 Given a scenario, apply common security techniques to computing resources.

Password management would not necessarily prevent the insecure storage of passwords. It would require employees to use the management system consistently.

flashcards.term: Place the IDS directly behind the router
flashcards.definition: Placing the IDS directly behind the router ensures visibility of all incoming and outgoing traffic, which is crucial for detecting any unusual patterns or potential threats. Placing the IDS outside the firewall exposes it to all internet traffic, including a lot of noise and potential direct attacks on the IDS itself, which is not optimal.
topics: 4.5 Given a scenario, modify enterprise capabilities to enhance security.
flashcards.hint: Placing the IDS directly behind the router ensures visibility of all incoming and outgoing traffic, which is crucial for detecting any unusual patterns or potential threats.
flashcards.front: Placing the IDS directly behind the router ensures visibility of all incoming and outgoing traffic, which is crucial for detecting any unusual patterns or potential threats.
flashcards.keyDetails: Placing the IDS outside the firewall exposes it to all internet traffic, including a lot of noise and potential direct attacks on the IDS itself, which is not optimal. Placing the IDS at the end of the network could result in it only seeing outbound traffic, thus limiting its visibility and effectiveness.
flashcards.example: A company has expanded its operations to a new location and is setting up its network infrastructure.

flashcards.definition: Plaintext refers to data that is not encrypted. An attacker can easily intercept data that is in plaintext form.
topics: 4.1 Given a scenario, apply common security techniques to computing resources.
flashcards.hint: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.
flashcards.front: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.
flashcards.keyDetails: An attacker can easily intercept data that is in plaintext form. Ciphertext refers to data that went through an encryption algorithm. Creating ciphertext makes it less likely for third parties to intercept and read. An algorithm refers to the operations that transform plaintext into ciphertext with cryptographic properties, also called a cipher.
flashcards.example: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.

flashcards.term: Signature-based detection relies on known attack patterns, while anomaly-based detection identifies deviations from normal behavior.
flashcards.definition: Signature-based detection uses known patterns of malicious behavior (or "signatures") to identify potential threats. This method can quickly and accurately detect known threats but may fail to identify new, unrecorded types of attacks.
topics: Imported multiple-choice questions from A.2 Security+ Final Assessment (source question 47)
flashcards.hint: How does signature-based detection differ from anomaly-based detection in an intrusion detection system (IDS)?
flashcards.front: How does signature-based detection differ from anomaly-based detection in an intrusion detection system (IDS)?
flashcards.keyDetails: This method can quickly and accurately detect known threats but may fail to identify new, unrecorded types of attacks. In contrast, anomaly-based detection works by defining what's considered "normal" behavior within a system and then alerting on any behavior that deviates from this norm.
flashcards.example: How does signature-based detection differ from anomaly-based detection in an intrusion detection system (IDS)?

flashcards.definition: Zero-day vulnerabilities refer to previously unknown software or hardware flaws that attackers can exploit before developers or vendors become aware of or have a chance to fix them. flashcards.definition: A Certificate Authority (CA) is a server that guarantees subject identities by issuing signed digital certificate wrappers for their public keys. The Root of Trust model defines how users and different CAs can trust one another.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
flashcards.hint: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.
flashcards.front: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.
flashcards.keyDetails: The Root of Trust model defines how users and different CAs can trust one another. Each CA issues itself a certificate known as a Root Certificate. The Root Certificate is self-signed, meaning the CA server signs a certificate issued to itself. A subject must complete a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) and submit it to the CA to get a certificate.
flashcards.example: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.

flashcards.keyDetails: Data protection is a component of ZTA that controls and secures access to sensitive data, including encryption and auditing. Better access controls are a benefit of using a ZTA but are not considered a component. They include more stringent limits regarding who or what can access resources and from what locations.

flashcards.term: Discontinue the use of the software to avoid the risks

topics: 5.3 Explain the processes associated with third-party risk assessment and management.
flashcards.hint: Implementing new security controls is an appropriate response as it involves taking proactive steps to reduce the impact or likelihood of the risk. Purchasing cybersecurity insurance to transfer the risks is also an appropriate response.

flashcards.keyDetails: Purchasing cybersecurity insurance to transfer the risks is also an appropriate response. It involves assigning the risk to a third party, in this case, an insurance company. Discontinuing the use of the software to avoid risks is another appropriate response. It involves stopping the activity causing the risk, thus eliminating its source.

flashcards.term: Implement data classification based on sensitivity
flashcards.definition: Organizations use regular audits to identify the location of their data storage, determine who has access, and assess if they adequately protect it. This proactive approach allows for early detection of vulnerabilities.

flashcards.definition: Implementing network segmentation and isolation is an effective way to minimize the attack surface. flashcards.term: Implement new security controls to mitigate the risks

flashcards.front: Implementing new security controls is an appropriate response as it involves taking proactive steps to reduce the impact or likelihood of the risk.
flashcards.keyDetails: Purchasing cybersecurity insurance to transfer the risks is also an appropriate response. It involves assigning the risk to a third party, in this case, an insurance company. Discontinuing the use of the software to avoid risks is another appropriate response. It involves stopping the activity causing the risk, thus eliminating its source.

flashcards.term: Implement the principle of least privilege
flashcards.definition: Implementing the principle of least privilege ensures that users, systems, and processes have the minimum privileges needed to perform their tasks, thus reducing the attack surface. Installing the latest antivirus software helps to detect and remove malicious software but does not necessarily limit the number of vulnerabilities an attacker can exploit.

Increasing the complexity of user passwords is part of a robust password policy, but it does not reduce the attack surface.

flashcards.term: Limiting the scope and magnitude of the incident
flashcards.definition: During the containment phase of the incident response process, the primary goal is to limit the scope and magnitude of the incident, which includes securing data and minimizing the immediate impact on customers and business partners. The company's system has already identified the indicators of threat actor activity, meaning this scenario has already passed.
topics: 5.1 Summarize elements of effective security governance.
flashcards.hint: During the containment phase of the incident response process, the primary goal is to limit the scope and magnitude of the incident, which includes securing data and minimizing the immediate impact on customers and business partners.
flashcards.front: During the containment phase of the incident response process, the primary goal is to limit the scope and magnitude of the incident, which includes securing data and minimizing the immediate impact on customers and business partners.
flashcards.keyDetails: The company's system has already identified the indicators of threat actor activity, meaning this scenario has already passed. This action corresponds to the eradication phase, which comes after the containment phase in the incident response process.
flashcards.example: The company's system has recently detected suspicious network activity, signaling a possible cybersecurity incident.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Limiting" + "incident" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: The MTTR is 6 hours, which is the time it took to repair and restore operations for each failure.
topics: 5.3 Explain the processes associated with third-party risk assessment and management.
flashcards.hint: The MTTR is 6 hours, which is the time it took to repair and restore operations for each failure.
flashcards.front: The MTTR is 6 hours, which is the time it took to repair and restore operations for each failure.
flashcards.keyDetails: The MTBF is 2,920 hours/failure, calculated by dividing the total operational time of 8,760 hours per year by 3 failures. While the MTTR is correct (average time to repair a failure which is 6 hours), the MTBF is incorrect. MTBF is the total operational time divided by the number of failures. Both MTTR (2) and MTBF (1,000) are incorrect.

flashcards.definition: Organizations actively create a balance between enhanced security and manageable complexity when they deploy Unified Threat Management devices internally while also maintaining Next Generation Firewalls at network boundaries. flashcards.term: Penetration testers identify vulnerabilities to improve security.

flashcards.term: Perform regular audits of asset inventory

flashcards.term: Purchase cybersecurity insurance to transfer the risks
flashcards.definition: Implementing new security controls is an appropriate response as it involves taking proactive steps to reduce the impact or likelihood of the risk. Purchasing cybersecurity insurance to transfer the risks is also an appropriate response.
topics: 5.3 Explain the processes associated with third-party risk assessment and management.
flashcards.hint: Implementing new security controls is an appropriate response as it involves taking proactive steps to reduce the impact or likelihood of the risk. Purchasing cybersecurity insurance to transfer the risks is also an appropriate response.
flashcards.front: Implementing new security controls is an appropriate response as it involves taking proactive steps to reduce the impact or likelihood of the risk. Purchasing cybersecurity insurance to transfer the risks is also an appropriate response.
flashcards.keyDetails: Purchasing cybersecurity insurance to transfer the risks is also an appropriate response. It involves assigning the risk to a third party, in this case, an insurance company. Discontinuing the use of the software to avoid risks is another appropriate response. It involves stopping the activity causing the risk, thus eliminating its source.
flashcards.example: A company is experiencing increased cybersecurity risks due to a new software application it developed for inventory management.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Purchase" + "risks" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Regulated data refers to specific categories of information subject to legal or regulatory requirements regarding their handling, storage, and protection, which typically includes sensitive or personally identifiable information (PII), such as healthcare records and social security numbers. flashcards.definition: In this scenario, the best option is to use a symmetric algorithm, as it uses the same secret key to perform encryption and decryption and allows for efficiency, security and speed. Symmetric encryption is used for bulk encryption of large amounts of data.
topics: 5.3 Explain the processes associated with third-party risk assessment and management.
flashcards.hint: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.
flashcards.front: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.
flashcards.keyDetails: Symmetric encryption is used for bulk encryption of large amounts of data. An asymmetric algorithm performs both encryption and decryption, but with two different but related public and private keys in a key pair. Plaintext refers to data that is not encrypted. Anyone can easily intercept data that is in plaintext form.
flashcards.example: A security team is evaluating cryptographic protections and must choose the method or component that best fits the need.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Symmetric" + "algorithm" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: The employee percentage who have received compliance training
flashcards.definition: The percentage of employees who have undergone compliance training is the most relevant for managing regulatory compliance risk. Training employees on the new regulatory requirements ensures that they understand the rules and can act in compliance with them.
topics: 5.6 Given a scenario, implement security awareness practices.
flashcards.hint: The percentage of employees who have undergone compliance training is the most relevant for managing regulatory compliance risk. Training employees on the new regulatory requirements ensures that they understand the rules and can act in compliance with them.
flashcards.front: The percentage of employees who have undergone compliance training is the most relevant for managing regulatory compliance risk. Training employees on the new regulatory requirements ensures that they understand the rules and can act in compliance with them.
flashcards.keyDetails: Training employees on the new regulatory requirements ensures that they understand the rules and can act in compliance with them. While revenue is an important business metric, it does not directly measure regulatory compliance risk.
flashcards.example: An organization is expanding its operations into a new region with unfamiliar regulatory requirements.
flashcards.memoryHook: "The" + "training" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: The order of volatility ensures evidence from volatile sources gets collected before less volatile sources.
flashcards.definition: To prevent data loss, prioritize collecting evidence from volatile sources like CPU registers, cache memory, and system memory before moving on to less volatile sources. The order of volatility pertains to the sequence of collecting data based on its volatility level and has no direct correlation with the legality of data seizure.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
flashcards.hint: To prevent data loss, prioritize collecting evidence from volatile sources like CPU registers, cache memory, and system memory before moving on to less volatile sources.
flashcards.front: To prevent data loss, prioritize collecting evidence from volatile sources like CPU registers, cache memory, and system memory before moving on to less volatile sources.
flashcards.keyDetails: The order of volatility pertains to the sequence of collecting data based on its volatility level and has no direct correlation with the legality of data seizure.
flashcards.example: An incident response team is working through a case and must choose the next best response or investigative action.
flashcards.memoryHook: "The" + "sources." → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: The organization may struggle with managing multiple cloud environments and enforcing consistent security policies.
flashcards.definition: A hybrid cloud model can present security challenges, including the complexity of managing multiple cloud environments and enforcing consistent security policies across all environments. In a hybrid cloud model, the organization can control the security of its data and applications in the private cloud infrastructure.
topics: 5.1 Summarize elements of effective security governance.
flashcards.hint: A hybrid cloud model can present security challenges, including the complexity of managing multiple cloud environments and enforcing consistent security policies across all environments. In a hybrid cloud model, the organization can control the security of its data and applications in the private cloud infrastructure.
flashcards.front: A hybrid cloud model can present security challenges, including the complexity of managing multiple cloud environments and enforcing consistent security policies across all environments. In a hybrid cloud model, the organization can control the security of its data and applications in the private cloud infrastructure.
flashcards.keyDetails: In a hybrid cloud model, the organization can control the security of its data and applications in the private cloud infrastructure. However, it needs to ensure that the public cloud provider also has robust security measures.
flashcards.example: A large organization is planning to move its operations to the cloud and is considering different cloud deployment models.
flashcards.memoryHook: "The" + "policies." → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: The potential impact of the vulnerability on the organization's core operations
flashcards.definition: Organizations primarily consider the potential operational impact when prioritizing vulnerabilities.
topics: 5.5 Explain types and purposes of audits and assessments.
flashcards.hint: Organizations primarily consider the potential operational impact when prioritizing vulnerabilities.
flashcards.front: Organizations primarily consider the potential operational impact when prioritizing vulnerabilities.
flashcards.keyDetails: They should urgently address exploitations that could cause severe operational disruptions, data breaches, or other significant problems. Older software may be more vulnerable, but its age does not primarily dictate prioritization. Instead, the vulnerability's severity and potential impact hold greater importance.
flashcards.example: The security team at a major corporation has discovered multiple vulnerabilities during its latest assessment.
flashcards.memoryHook: "The" + "operations" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: The risk to the users is significant, and the vendor should quietly create a patch without informing the users until it is ready.
flashcards.definition: Zero-day vulnerabilities represent significant risk, and the vendor should prioritize creating a patch.
topics: 5.3 Explain the processes associated with third-party risk assessment and management.
flashcards.hint: Zero-day vulnerabilities represent significant risk, and the vendor should prioritize creating a patch.
flashcards.front: Zero-day vulnerabilities represent significant risk, and the vendor should prioritize creating a patch.
flashcards.keyDetails: Disclosing the vulnerability to the public before a patch is ready could increase the risk. Alerting users about the vulnerability could also alert potential threat actors, increasing the risk before a patch is ready.
flashcards.example: A major software vendor becomes aware of a new zero-day vulnerability in one of its products due to an anonymous tip.
flashcards.memoryHook: "The" + "ready." → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: The vendor's financial stability, operational reliability, and data security practices
flashcards.definition: Financial stability, operational reliability, data security, and regulatory compliance are critical factors in evaluating a vendor's risk profile. These considerations directly relate to the potential risks the vendor might introduce to the organization, including financial risks, operational disruptions, data breaches, and regulatory violations.
topics: 5.3 Explain the processes associated with third-party risk assessment and management.
flashcards.hint: Financial stability, operational reliability, data security, and regulatory compliance are critical factors in evaluating a vendor's risk profile.
flashcards.front: Financial stability, operational reliability, data security, and regulatory compliance are critical factors in evaluating a vendor's risk profile.
flashcards.keyDetails: These considerations directly relate to the potential risks the vendor might introduce to the organization, including financial risks, operational disruptions, data breaches, and regulatory violations. While reputation and branding can indicate a vendor's quality and reliability, they are not directly related to risk.
flashcards.example: An organization plans to outsource its customer service operations to a third-party vendor.
flashcards.memoryHook: "The" + "practices" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Threat hunting is the best match here because it names proactively searching for threats that have not triggered alerts.
topics: 5.2 Explain elements of the risk management process.
flashcards.hint: A security analyst is documenting proactively searching for threats that have not triggered alerts.
flashcards.front: A security analyst is documenting proactively searching for threats that have not triggered alerts.
flashcards.keyDetails: The other options describe different ideas.
flashcards.example: A security analyst is documenting proactively searching for threats that have not triggered alerts.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Threat" + "hunting" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: A time-based restriction would prevent access to corporate resources outside a set schedule. The company should set this account policy.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
flashcards.hint: A time-based restriction would prevent access to corporate resources outside a set schedule. The company should set this account policy. Location-based access policies would need a temporary exemption option to allow for travel.
flashcards.front: A time-based restriction would prevent access to corporate resources outside a set schedule. The company should set this account policy. Location-based access policies would need a temporary exemption option to allow for travel.
flashcards.keyDetails: The company should set this account policy. Location-based access policies would need a temporary exemption option to allow for travel. Location-based access policies prevent access to company systems outside a specified area (typically the company's state).
flashcards.example: An organization is designing access and identity controls and must choose the authentication method or relationship that best fits the requirement.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Time-based" + "restrictions" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: To define specific requirements and project deliverables expected from the vendor
flashcards.definition: Its primary purpose is to define specific requirements and project deliverables clearly.
topics: Imported multiple-choice questions from A.2 Security+ Final Assessment (source question 13)
flashcards.hint: Its primary purpose is to define specific requirements and project deliverables clearly.
flashcards.front: Its primary purpose is to define specific requirements and project deliverables clearly.
flashcards.keyDetails: Although the vendor selection process involves feedback and evaluations, the WO and SOW's focus is specifying the project's requirements and deliverables. Pricing limitations by the WO or SOW do not apply to the vendor. Instead, its main objective is to outline the work and the desired outcomes without dictating pricing terms.
flashcards.example: In a tech company, the IT department is selecting a new vendor to upgrade its network infrastructure.
flashcards.memoryHook: "To" + "vendor" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: To define the scope of the assessment, testing methods, and timeframe for conducting the test
flashcards.definition: Establishing ROE in a penetration testing engagement is essential to define the assessment's scope, testing methods, and timeframe.
topics: Imported multiple-choice questions from A.2 Security+ Final Assessment (source question 34)
flashcards.hint: Establishing ROE in a penetration testing engagement is essential to define the assessment's scope, testing methods, and timeframe.
flashcards.front: Establishing ROE in a penetration testing engagement is essential to define the assessment's scope, testing methods, and timeframe.
flashcards.keyDetails: The IT team uses ROE to prevent unintended disruptions to critical services and ensure a smooth testing process. Although collaboration is important, the primary purpose of ROE is to establish the parameters and scope of the testing.
flashcards.example: The IT department in an accounting firm is gearing up for an external penetration testing engagement to evaluate the organization's security readiness.
flashcards.memoryHook: "To" + "test" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: To proactively discover evidence of known TTPs and indicators within the network.
flashcards.definition: Threat hunting involves actively searching for signs of known tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) and network-compromised indicators. It allows organizations to take preemptive actions to prevent security incidents and breaches.
topics: 5.3 Explain the processes associated with third-party risk assessment and management.
flashcards.hint: Threat hunting involves actively searching for signs of known tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) and network-compromised indicators. It allows organizations to take preemptive actions to prevent security incidents and breaches.
flashcards.front: Threat hunting involves actively searching for signs of known tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) and network-compromised indicators. It allows organizations to take preemptive actions to prevent security incidents and breaches.
flashcards.keyDetails: It allows organizations to take preemptive actions to prevent security incidents and breaches. Threat hunting is a proactive approach that involves searching for potential threats before they trigger alerts. It does not rely solely on reactive responses to incident management system alerts.
flashcards.example: An incident response team is working through a case and must choose the next best response or investigative action.
flashcards.memoryHook: "To" + "network." → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: TOCTOU stands for Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use. A race condition caused by a state change between checking a resource and using it.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
flashcards.hint: A security analyst is documenting a race condition caused by a state change between checking a resource and using it.
flashcards.front: A security analyst is documenting a race condition caused by a state change between checking a resource and using it.
flashcards.keyDetails: a race condition caused by a state change between checking a resource and using it.
flashcards.example: A security analyst is documenting a race condition caused by a state change between checking a resource and using it.

flashcards.definition: Trade secrets refer to confidential business information that provides a competitive edge.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
5.5 Explain types and purposes of audits and assessments.
flashcards.hint: A compliance team is reviewing data handling, privacy, or retention requirements and must choose the best concept.
flashcards.front: A compliance team is reviewing data handling, privacy, or retention requirements and must choose the best concept.
flashcards.keyDetails: Trade secret data refers to valuable, confidential information that gives a business a competitive advantage. Human-readable data is information that humans can easily understand and interpret without additional processing or translation.
flashcards.example: A compliance team is reviewing data handling, privacy, or retention requirements and must choose the best concept.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Trade" + "secrets" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: Unauthorized access becomes easier for potential attackers.
flashcards.definition: Non-updated legacy firmware remains unprotected from patches designed to rectify known vulnerabilities, making it an inviting target for attackers who can exploit these weak spots to achieve unauthorized system access. Outdated firmware does not magically enhance its performance over time.
topics: 5.6 Given a scenario, implement security awareness practices.
flashcards.hint: Non-updated legacy firmware remains unprotected from patches designed to rectify known vulnerabilities, making it an inviting target for attackers who can exploit these weak spots to achieve unauthorized system access. Outdated firmware does not magically enhance its performance over time.
flashcards.front: Non-updated legacy firmware remains unprotected from patches designed to rectify known vulnerabilities, making it an inviting target for attackers who can exploit these weak spots to achieve unauthorized system access. Outdated firmware does not magically enhance its performance over time.
flashcards.keyDetails: Outdated firmware does not magically enhance its performance over time. In fact, as technology advances, it tends to become less compatible and loses efficiency when juxtaposed against its updated counterparts.
flashcards.example: A software engineer at a growing tech company identifies that some divisions in the organization still operate on legacy systems.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Unauthorized" + "attackers." → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Wi-Fi Protected Access 3 (WPA3) enhances security beyond its predecessors and is the most robust wireless data protection encryption standard. It rectifies vulnerabilities found in WPA2 and represents the latest standard.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
flashcards.hint: A company's IT security specialist decides to upgrade the wireless network infrastructure to enhance data protection during transmissions. Recognizing the importance of strong encryption for wireless data, the specialist evaluates the various encryption standards available.
flashcards.front: A company's IT security specialist decides to upgrade the wireless network infrastructure to enhance data protection during transmissions. Recognizing the importance of strong encryption for wireless data, the specialist evaluates the various encryption standards available.
flashcards.keyDetails: It rectifies vulnerabilities found in WPA2 and represents the latest standard. People view wired equivalent privacy (WEP) as a dated wireless encryption standard and deem it weak due to its identifiable vulnerabilities. It does not measure up to WPA3 in security.
flashcards.example: A company's IT security specialist decides to upgrade the wireless network infrastructure to enhance data protection during transmissions.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Wi-Fi" + "3" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Wi-Fi Protected Access 3 (WPA3)-Enterprise provides robust security and supports authentication methods that validate both the device and the user, such as Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP). While WPA3 Pre-shared Key (WPA3-PSK) offers enhanced security over its predecessors, it does not distinctly validate device identity, and its foundation was originally a shared passphrase that generated the key to encrypt communications for a group of users.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
flashcards.hint: Wi-Fi Protected Access 3 (WPA3)-Enterprise provides robust security and supports authentication methods that validate both the device and the user, such as Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP).
flashcards.front: Wi-Fi Protected Access 3 (WPA3)-Enterprise provides robust security and supports authentication methods that validate both the device and the user, such as Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP).
flashcards.keyDetails: While WPA3 Pre-shared Key (WPA3-PSK) offers enhanced security over its predecessors, it does not distinctly validate device identity, and its foundation was originally a shared passphrase that generated the key to encrypt communications for a group of users.
flashcards.example: A medium-sized enterprise is revamping its wireless network infrastructure to improve security.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Wi-Fi" + "3-Enterprise" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: Wired equivalent privacy (WEP) is an outdated encryption standard considered weak due to known vulnerabilities.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
flashcards.hint: A medium-sized enterprise is revamping its wireless network infrastructure to improve security. The IT manager decided to update the company's Wi-Fi authentication method as the first step.
flashcards.front: A medium-sized enterprise is revamping its wireless network infrastructure to improve security. The IT manager decided to update the company's Wi-Fi authentication method as the first step.
flashcards.keyDetails: While WPA3 Pre-shared Key (WPA3-PSK) offers enhanced security over its predecessors, it does not distinctly validate device identity, and its foundation was originally a shared passphrase that generated the key to encrypt communications for a group of users.
flashcards.example: A medium-sized enterprise is revamping its wireless network infrastructure to improve security.
flashcards.memoryHook: "Wired" + "Privacy" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.term: Within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach
flashcards.definition: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) requires that an organization must report a breach of personal data to the relevant supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovering it unless the breach is unlikely to risk the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
topics: 5.4 Summarize elements of effective security compliance.
flashcards.hint: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) requires that an organization must report a breach of personal data to the relevant supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovering it unless the breach is unlikely to risk the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
flashcards.front: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) requires that an organization must report a breach of personal data to the relevant supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovering it unless the breach is unlikely to risk the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
flashcards.keyDetails: GDPR requires that organizations must report personal data breaches within 72 hours, not 24. Delaying notification beyond the mandated timeframe could result in penalties. A time frame of 96 hours is longer than the period specified by GDPR. The regulation stipulates that organizations must report breaches within 72 hours of becoming aware of them.
flashcards.example: Under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), how soon must an organization report a breach of personal data?
flashcards.memoryHook: "Within" + "breach" → connect the words to the definition.

flashcards.definition: XDR stands for Extended Detection and Response. XDR is the best match here because it names threat detection and response that correlates telemetry across multiple security layers.
topics: 5.5 Explain types and purposes of audits and assessments.
flashcards.hint: A security analyst is documenting threat detection and response that correlates telemetry across multiple security layers.
flashcards.front: A security analyst is documenting threat detection and response that correlates telemetry across multiple security layers.
flashcards.keyDetails: The other options describe different ideas.
flashcards.example: A security analyst is documenting threat detection and response that correlates telemetry across multiple security layers.
